title: DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY 95,154-162 (1983) The Effect of Folic Acid on CAMP-Elicited CAMP Production in Dictyostelium discoidem
text:
DEVELOPMENTAL 
BIOLOGY 
95,154-162 
(1983) 
The Effect of Folic Acid on CAMP-Elicited CAMP 
Production in Dictyostelium discoidem 
PETER N. DEVREOTES 
Department 
of Physiological 
Chemistry, 
Johns Hopkins 
University 
School of Medicine, 
725 N. Wolfe Street, Baltimore, 
Maryland 
21205 
Received March 
23, 1982; accepted in revised form August 
27, 1982 
In Dictyostelium 
discoideum, 
folic 
acid serves as a chemoattractant, 
accelerates 
the appearance 
of several 
devel- 
opmentally 
regulated 
enzymes, alters 
the phase of spontaneous 
light-scattering 
oscillations, 
and elicits 
increases 
in 
cGMP and CAMP. The effect of folic acid and pteridine 
analogs on rates of [3H]cAMP 
secretion, 
monitored 
by rapid 
perfusion, 
was studied. At low cell densities 
folic acid elicited 
only minimal 
[aH]cAMP 
secretion. 
At high cell densities, 
folic acid elicited 
greater 
increases 
in cellular 
CAMP and rates of [3H]cAMP 
secretion 
than did maximally 
effective 
doses of CAMP. Following 
pretreatment 
with 10m8 M CAMP for 10 min or 1Om6 M CAMP for 2 min, no rH]cAMP 
was 
secreted in response to folic acid. Conversely, 
pretreatment 
with folic acid did not impair 
subsequent 
responsiveness 
to 5 X 10e9 M CAMP. Simultaneous 
addition 
of folic acid and CAMP increased the release of [aH]cAMP 
two- to threefold 
compared 
to control 
stimuli 
of the same concentration 
of CAMP. Pretreatment 
with a mixed stimulus 
attenuated 
the 
response to a subsequent increment 
in the CAMP concentration 
only if the higher CAMP stimulus 
did not include folic 
acid. The effects of folic acid are mimicked 
by the pteridine 
ring alone; analogs substituted 
at the 2- or 5-position 
are 
ineffective; 
non-reducible 
analogs are partially 
active. The observations 
presented 
here suggest significant 
differences 
in the mechanisms 
by which 
CAMP and folic 
acid stimulate 
CAMP production. 
Rather 
than there being separate 
receptors 
for each chemoattractant 
that interact 
in the same way with an intracellular 
pathway, 
it appears that folic 
acid and pteridine 
analogs potentiate 
the response to CAMP. 
INTRODUCTION 
Dictyostelium discoideum grows as free-living 
amoe- 
bae, until 
the nutrient 
supply 
is exhausted 
or re- 
moved. Starvation 
triggers 
a developmental 
program; 
initially 
the amoebae become specialized 
to communi- 
cate via extracellular 
3’,5’-cyclic adenosine 
monophos- 
phate (CAMP). Chemotaxis 
toward 
the nucleotide 
me- 
diates the aggregation 
of up to lo6 cells to form mul- 
ticellular 
structures. 
Time-lapse 
observation 
of this 
highly 
coordinated 
process suggests that 
the chemo- 
tactic signals 
are relayed 
through 
the monolayer 
for 
long distances 
(up to 3 cm). Previous 
work has shown 
that isolated 
amoebae are attracted 
to CAMP and se- 
crete CAMP in response to exogenous 
CAMP stimuli 
(Konijn 
et al., 1968; Shaffer, 
1975; Roos et al., 1975). 
Furthermore, 
waves of CAMP, which emanate from cen- 
ters with a regular periodicity 
of 5 to 10 min have been 
visualized 
in monolayers 
of aggregating 
amoebae (Tom- 
chik and Devreotes, 1981; reviewed in Devreotes, 1982). 
The CAMP-signaling 
response 
(i.e., 
CAMP-elicited 
CAMP secretion) 
has been extensively 
characterized. 
Figure 1 illustrates 
the interaction 
of the components 
which generate the CAMP-signaling 
response. A change 
in occupancy of surface CAMP receptors 
leads to acti- 
vation of adenylate cyclase (V, in Fig. 1) and an ensuing 
accumulation 
of intracellular 
CAMP (Roos et al., 1975; 
Dinauer 
et al., 1980a; Gerisch and Wick, 1975). Not all 
of the newly synthesized 
CAMP can be accounted 
for 
in the extracellular 
medium, suggesting 
that a fraction 
is degraded 
by intracellular 
phosphodiesterase 
(Di- 
nauer et aZ., 1980a). The CAMP secretion rate is directly 
proportional 
to the intracellular 
CAMP level; there is 
no long-term 
storage of newly synthesized 
CAMP (Ger- 
isch and Wick, 1975; Dinauer et al., 1980a). After several 
minutes, the rate of CAMP synthesis 
decreases (see be- 
low) and the remaining 
CAMP is degraded by extra- 
cellular 
and membrane-bound 
phosphodiesterase. 
Interaction 
of extracellular 
CAMP with surface CAMP 
receptors can create a positive 
feedback loop since the 
secreted 
CAMP adds to the stimulus. 
Extracellular 
phosphodiesterase 
counteracts 
this feedback 
loop by 
decreasing 
extracellular 
CAMP; removal 
(or decrease) 
of the CAMP stimulus 
during 
a CAMP-signaling 
re- 
sponse leads to the abrupt termination 
of the response 
(Devreotes and Steck, 1979). The opposing effects of the 
positive 
feedback loop and phosphodiesterase 
suggest 
the system can exhibit 
excitable 
behavior. 
Evidence for 
this excitability 
is found when the cells are studied in 
suspension 
(and presumably 
occurs in situ on an agar 
surface). For instance, at critical 
times in development 
CAMP levels oscillate spontaneously 
(Gerisch and Hess, 
1974; Gerisch 
and Wick, 1975). Furthermore, 
there is 
a discrete “refractory 
period” 
before a second reponse 
can be initiated. 
The effects of both the positive feedback loop and the 
extracellular 
phosphodiesterase 
can be minimized 
if the 
cells are stimulated 
under conditions 
of rapid perfusion.

discoideum

extracellular 
intracellular 
and membrane 
phosphodiesterase 
phosphodiesterase 
FIG. 1. Interaction 
of components 
involved 
in generation 
of CAMP signaling 
response. Activity 
of adenylate 
cyclase, V, is modulated 
by 
occupancy of surface receptors. 
Rate of CAMP secretion 
is directly 
proportional 
to intracellular 
concentration 
(ks is first-order 
rate constant). 
Dotted 
line represents 
autocatalytic 
feedback 
loop whereby 
secreted CAMP can serve as an additional 
stimulus. 
Exogenously 
added CAMP 
is also subject to degradation 
by extracellular 
and membrane 
phosphodiesterases. 
Secreted CAMP is rapidly 
removed; degraded CAMP is 
rapidly 
renewed. 
In this way, extracellular 
CAMP is 
“clamped” 
at the concentration 
of the applied stimulus 
(Devreotes 
et al., 1979). However, 
even when a CAMP 
stimulus 
is held constant, 
the activation 
of adenylate 
cyclase is transient. 
The intracellular 
CAMP level and 
the rate of CAMP secretion 
peak after 2 min of stim- 
ulation 
and then fall to basal values after 5-10 min. The 
mechanism 
by which 
amoebae terminate 
their 
signal- 
ing response to extracellular 
CAMP is referred 
to as 
adaptation. 
This mechanism 
presumably 
serves to pre- 
vent cells from responding 
indefinitely 
to their own se- 
cretions, 
which 
would 
disrupt 
intercellular 
signaling. 
Adaptation 
appears to reflect the adjustment 
of cell 
sensitivity 
to the ambient 
concentration 
of extracellu- 
lar CAMP in that adapted cells become unresponsive 
to 
a given CAMP stimulus, 
:yet respond to further 
increases 
in the external 
CAMP concentration. 
Adaptation 
per- 
sists as long as extracellular 
CAMP is present, but can 
be reversed by removing 
the stimulus. 
Folic acid (pteroylglutamic 
acid) has been implicated 
as a chemoattractant 
in the early developmental 
stages 
of D. discoideum 
and other cellular 
slime molds (Pan 
et al., 1972, 1975). As the cells differentiate, 
folic acid 
deaminase appears in parallel 
with 
the appearance 
of 
extracellular 
CAMP phoisphodiesterase 
(Pan and Wurs- 
ter, 1978; Bernstein 
and Van Driel, 1980a,b). Folic acid 
and CAMP each have been shown to induce the preco- 
cious appearance of theise enzymes (Klein, 
1975; Tsang 
and Coukell, 
1977; Bernstein 
and Van Driel, 
1980a,b; 
Bernstein 
et al., 1981). Addition 
of nanomolar 
to mi- 
cromolar 
amounts 
of folic acid (or CAMP) at 5- to 7- 
min intervals 
accelerates 
the appearance 
of sponta- 
neous oscillations 
in cell.ular CAMP levels. The phase of 
these spontaneous 
oscillations 
is altered 
in a similar 
manner by each of the chemoattractants 
(Wurster 
and 
Schubiger, 1977; Wurster et al., 1977; Gerisch et al., 1977). 
Folic acid also elicits increases in cellular 
cGMP levels 
and light-scattering 
responses (i.e., stimulus-induced 
decreases in optical 
density) 
in suspensions 
of D. dis- 
coideum and related species (Mato et al., 1977; Wurster 
and Schubiger, 
1977; Wurster 
et al., 1978). In addition 
it was shown that folic acid can elicit large changes in 
cellular 
CAMP levels (Wurster 
et al., 1978). The latter 
finding 
led to the experiments 
reported 
here. 
Based on the apparent 
similarities 
of many of the 
effects of folic acid and CAMP, it has been proposed that 
cell surface 
receptors 
for folic acid interact 
with 
an 
intracellular 
pathway 
similar 
to that linked to cell sur- 
face CAMP receptors (Bernstein 
et al., 1981). Indeed, the 
association 
of [“Hlfolic 
acid and [3H]methotrexate 
with 
D. discoideum 
cells has been measured 
(Wurster 
and 
Butz, 1980; Van Driel, 
1981 Nandini-Kishore 
and Fra- 
zier, 1982). In an effort to determine 
the extent of over- 
lap of the pathways 
for activation 
of adenylate 
cyclase 
by folic acid and CAMP, CAMP synthesis 
and secretion 
elicited 
by each of these chemoattractants 
were com- 
pared. It is clear from the results presented below that 
the mechanisms 
by which 
folic acid and CAMP elicit 
the response differ 
dramatically. 
Folic 
acid provides 
only a minimal 
(perhaps absent) stimulus 
and even the 
highest 
concentrations 
elicit 
little 
or no adaptation. 
Rather than directly 
activating 
the adenylate 
cyclase, 
it appears that folic acid potentiates 
the stimulatory 
effect of CAMP. 
METHODS 
Cell Culture and Development 
D. discoideum strain NC-4 was grown on SM nutrient 
agar [lo g bactopeptone, 
10 g dextrose, 1 g yeast extract, 
per liter 
development 
buffer 
(5 mM Na2HP04, 
5 mM 
KH2P04, 1 mM MgCl,, 
200 PM CaCl,)] to a density 
of

l-5 
X 10’ cells/loo-mm 
dish (Sussman, and Sussman, 
1967). No signs of clearing 
of the bacterial 
lawn were 
apparent when the cells were harvested. Cells were freed 
of bacteria by three to four washes (600g for 3 min) in 
development 
buffer. 
Strain 
Ax-3 was grown 
in HL-5 
medium, harvested 
at a density less than 5 X lo6 cells/ 
ml, and washed twice in development 
buffer. Cells were 
shaken at 150 rpm at 22°C for 3-5 hr. 
CAMP Levels in Cell Suspensions 
Ax-3 cells were washed once, suspended at a density 
of 10’ cells/ml 
in development 
buffer, and synchronized 
by addition 
of 2 X lop8 M CAMP (l/100 
volume 
of 2 
X lop6 M) at 6-min intervals. 
Cellular 
CAMP levels were 
monitored 
during 
the fifth 
and sixth 
cycles. Fifteen 
microliters 
of cell suspension 
was added to 15 yl of 2 
X lop7 M [3H]cAMP 
(NEN 38 Ci/mmole) 
in 5% TCA. 
Samples were cooled to 4°C and 80 ~1 of erythrocyte 
ghost suspension in 0.5 M potassium 
phosphate, pH 7.0,

RESULTS 
[3HjcAMP 
Secretion in Rapid Perfusion 
Folic Acid-Elicited 
CAMP Production 
and Secretion 
Washed NC-4 cells were prepared for measurements 
of [3H]cAMP 
secretion 
exactly 
as previously 
described 
(Dinauer 
et al., 1980a). In a few cases, development 
buffer was substituted 
for nonnutrient 
buffer. Ax-3 cells 
were fed [3H]adenosine-labeled 
Escherichia 
coli follow- 
ing the same protocol 
used for NC-4 cells. After 
3 to 5 
hr in shaken suspension, 
Ax-3 cells were freed of un- 
digested bacteria 
and added directly 
to the perfusion 
apparatus. 
In each case, cells were synchronized 
as pre- 
viously 
described (Dinauer 
et al., 1980a). 
Phosphodiesterase 
Activity 
Phosphodiesterase 
activity 
was measured by a mod- 
ification 
of the method of Brooks et al. (1968). Reaction 
mixtures 
contained 
4 X lop5 M [3H]cAMP, 
50 pg/ml 
5’- 
nucleotidase 
in 100 ~1 50 mM Tris-HCl, 
pH 7.5, 5 mM 
MgClz. The reaction 
was started 
by addition 
of loo-p1 
cells or extract. 
To stop the reaction, 
100 ~1 was added 
to 100 ~1 methanol 
and loaded on BioRad AG-2 columns 
(1 X 0.5 cm) equilibrated 
in methanol. 
Columns 
were 
eluted with 
600 ~1 methanol 
directly 
into scintillation 
vials. 
It was previously 
reported 
that suspensions 
of ag- 
gregation 
competent 
D. discoideum 
cells, strain 
AX-~, 
respond to the chemoattractant 
folic acid by elevating 
cellular 
CAMP levels. A similar 
response was elicited 
in shaken suspensions 
of strain 
Ax-3 following 
syn- 
chronization 
with 
nanomolar 
amounts 
of CAMP. The 
responses elicited 
by CAMP and by folic acid are com- 
pared in Fig. 2. Those elicited 
by folic acid were con- 
sistently 
larger 
(one- to threefold) 
and of longer du- 
ration 
(4 to 5 min compared to 2 to 3 min) than those 
elicited 
by CAMP. In a few instances, 
the peak CAMP 
level occurred 
1 min later when folate was used as a 
stimulus. 
The response elicited by CAMP plus folic acid 
was similar 
to that elicited 
by folic acid alone. 
What is the mechanism 
by which folic acid increases 
CAMP levels in cell suspensions? 
As illustrated 
in Fig. 
1, CAMP synthesis 
and secretion can be initiated 
by an 
abrupt 
increase in cellular 
sensitivity 
or inhibition 
of 
phosphodiesterase 
as well as by the customary 
stimulus 
(i.e., an increase in extracellular 
CAMP). Accordingly, 
folic acid may initiate 
a response by inhibition 
of phos- 
phodiesterase 
or enhancement 
of cellular 
sensitivity 
rather 
than by direct 
activation 
of adenylate 
cyclase.

[3H]cAMP

or high (5 X lo5 cells/filter) 
cell density.

(C) Amount 
of [3H]cAMP 
secreted in 13.5 min fol- 
lowing

of a 10m5 M CAMP (0) or 10m4 M folic

and (expressed as fraction 
of total 
radioac- 
tivity 
on filter 
(i.e., number of cells). 
In addition, 
the positive feedback loop amplifies 
the ini- 
tial reaction. 
Thus, the “response” 
elicited by folic acid 
in a cell suspension 
is necessarily 
a response to folic 
acid plus CAMP. To study the direct effect of folic acid 
on CAMP production, 
rates of [3H]cAMP secretion from 
sensitive 
cells were studied 
in a perfusion 
apparatus. 
The effects of both the positive feedback loop and phos- 
phodiesterase 
are minimized 
when cells are rapidly 
per- 
fused with the CAMP stimulus 
(Devreotes 
et al., 1979). 
The changes in [3H]cAMP secretion from aggregation 
competent 
Ax-3 cells during 
perfusion 
with 
folic acid 
depended on cell density, 
perfusion 
rate, and the par- 
ticular 
clone used. As shown in Fig. 3A, at low densities 
and high perfusion 
rates the rate of [3H]cAMP secretion 
increased only slightly 
vvhen folic acid was added to the 
perfusion 
buffer. 
The increased 
rate of [3H]cAMP 
se- 
cretion was about 3 to 5% of that elicited 
by CAMP. 
This response differed dramatically 
from that elicited 
from Ax-3 cells in suspension 
(i.e., Fig. 2). However, 
at 
high densities 
and slow rates of perfusion, 
folic 
acid 
elicited 
changes 
in [3H]cAMP 
secretion 
rates 
from 
strain Ax-3 that were reminiscent 
of those observed in 
cell suspensions 
(Figs. 3B, 4E, 5F). Often, the rate of 
[3H]cAMP secretion 
increased to a peak value at 3 min 
which 
was greater 
than the peak [3H]cAMP 
secretion 
rate elicited by 10m5 McAMP 
(Figs. 3B, 4D, E). The rate 
sometimes decreased to a level about half the peak value 
and oscillated 
about a plateau 
value. There was little 
tendency 
of the secretion 
rate to return 
toward 
and 
remain 
at the prestimulus 
value (i.e., no evidence of 
adaptation). 
In other Ax-3 clones the folic acid-elicited 
rate of [“HIcAMP 
secretion returned 
nearly to the pre- 
stimulus 
value after 8 to 10 min. As perfusion 
with folic 
acid was continued, 
a second increase in [3H]cAMP 
se- 
cretion often ensued (Devreotes, 
1982). Such a second- 
ary increase has never been observed during 
continued 
perfusion 
with CAMP. 
Figure 3C summarizes 
the cell density dependence of 
folic acid on CAMP-elicited 
[3H]cAMP 
secretion 
from 
one clone of strain AX-~. The magnitude 
of the response 
per cell was independent 
of cell density 
when CAMP 
was used as a stimulus. 
In contrast, 
the amount 
of 
[3H]cAMP 
secreted per cell depended markedly 
on cell 
density 
when folic acid was the stimulus. 
There was 
little 
response elicited 
by folic 
acid at low densities 
(cl.25 
X lo5 cells/cm2) while at slightly 
higher densities 
(~2.5 X lo5 cells/cm2), two to three times more [3H]cAMP 
was secreted in response to folic acid than in response 
to CAMP.

with CAMP on responsiveness 
to folic acid in strain AX-~. Cells were prepared 
and rates of [3H]cAMP secretion 
measured

Panels A and B are identical 
cultures 
from one experiment; 
panels C-F identical 
cultures 
from an 
independent 
experiment. 
A high cell density was used in each case (5 X lo5 cells/filter). 
Perfusion

(Fl) or CAMP (Cl) beginning

Cyclic AMP concentration 
was 10s M in panel B and 10m5 M in other panels. 
The strong dependence on cell density 
can be inter- 
preted according 
to the following 
hypothesis. 
The re- 
sponse to folic 
acid is dependent 
on secreted CAMP. 
With Ax-3 cells at lower densities 
(Fig. 3A), secreted 
CAMP is efficiently 
removed by rapid perfusion; 
folic 
acid alone has only a minimal 
effect. At higher densities 
(Figs. 3B, 4E), secreted CAMP quickly 
accumulates 
and 
serves as an additional 
stimulus 
which 
amplifies 
the 
initial 
effect of folic acid into a large “response.” 
This 
is also consistent 
with 
the observations 
of Ax-3 cells 
in suspension, 
where secreted CAMP can accumulate. 
The effect of folate there is to trigger 
a large increase 
in cellular 
CAMP; folate 
plus CAMP elicits 
the same 
increase (Fig. 2), 
NC-4 cells have less a tendency 
than Ax-3 to spon- 
taneously secrete CAMP and thus provide a cleaner sys- 
tem to study the response to an applied stimulus. 
Even 
at higher cell densities, rapid perfusion 
effectively 
con- 
trols the effects of the positive 
feedback and phospho- 
diesterase 
(Devreotes 
et al., 1979). Figures 
4A and B 
compare the rates of [3H]cAMP 
secretion 
from aggre- 
gation competent 
NC-4 cells elicited 
by lop5 M CAMP, 
a saturating 
dose, or by 10m4 M folic acid. Under con- 
ditions of rapid perfusion, 
the maximum 
effect of folic 
acid is only about 5% of that of CAMP. In seven inde- 
pendent experiments, 
the rate of [“HIcAMP 
secretion 
in response to folic reached a value of 5 to 15% that 
elicited 
by 10m5 M CAMP. Note again that when Ax-3 
cells at a similar 
density were perfused with folic acid 
the response is larger than that elicited by CAMP (Figs. 
4D, E; and Fig. 3). 
Efect 
of Pretreatment 
with CAMP on Responsiveness 
to Folic Acid 
It was next determined 
whether folic acid would elicit 
a response in cells that had been rendered less sensitive 
to CAMP by pretreatment 
with 
CAMP (i.e., adapted 
cells). Figure 
5B shows that 
following 
pretreatment 
with lop8 M CAMP, lop4 M folic no longer elicits an in- 
crease in [3H]cAMP secretion. In addition, 
2 min of pre- 
treatment 
with 
lop6 M CAMP was sufficient 
to extin- 
guish the effects of lop4 M folic acid (Figs. 5D and E) 
and a 1-min pretreatment 
significantly 
attenuated 
the 
effect (Fig. 5C). In contrast, 
lop4 M folic acid had little 
effect on subsequent 
responsiveness 
to CAMP (Fig. 5A 
and see below). 
Folic Acid Enhances CAMP-Elicited 
CAMP Secretion 
The data presented thus far indicate 
that when build 
up of extracellular 
CAMP is prevented, folic acid elicits 
only minimal 
increases in [3H]cAMP 
secretion. 
When 
cells are in close proximity 
these small increases can 
be amplified 
(i.e., secreted CAMP serves as an additional 
stimulus) 
into rates of [3H]cAMP secretion greater than 
those elicited 
by a saturating 
dose of CAMP. This sug- 
gests that 
folic 
acid increases 
cellular 
sensitivity 
to 
CAMP or otherwise 
enhances the response to CAMP. To 
directly 
demonstrate 
this effect, changes in [3H]cAMP 
secretion 
rates elicited 
by folic acid plus CAMP were 
compared to those elicited 
by CAMP alone. As shown 
in Figs. 4C, F and 6A-C, the combined stimulus 
elicited 
larger increases in the rate of [3H]cAMP secretion than

did CAMP alone. In addition, 
the increase elicited 
by 
the combined stimulus 
was frequently 
greater than the 
algebraic 
sum of the increases 
elicited 
by each che- 
moattractant 
individually 
(Figs. 4A-C). As illustrated 
in Fig. 6D, this effect was observed with every concen- 
tration 
of CAMP tested. The apparent 
fold-stimulation 
by folic acid was greatest at the lowest CAMP stimulus 
concentrations. 
In the limit 
of there being no CAMP 
stimulus

elicited

Eflect of Folic Acid on Adaptation 
The potentiation 
of the CAMP-elicited 
response by 
folic acid indicates that the combined stimulus 
provides 
a greater positive 
signal. to the adenylate 
cyclase. It is 
possible that the combined stimulus 
also raises cellular 
adaptation 
to a higher level. To test this possibility 
cells 
were stimulated 
with 
serial increments 
in CAMP con- 
centration 
in the absence and presence of folic acid. As 
shown in Figs. 6A and B, when the CAMP stimuli 
in- 
cluded folic acid, larger increases in [3H]cAMP secretion 
were observed in response to each increment. 
However, 
when the stimulus 
was switched from 4 X lop9 M CAMP 
plus folic acid to 2 X 10-s M CAMP, little 
increase in the 
CAMP secretion rate was observed (Fig. 6C). Pretreat- 
ment with 
folic acid plus the lower concentration 
of 
CAMP had apparently 
established 
a level of adaptation 
sufficient 
to significantly 
reduce an effect of the incre- 
ment in CAMP stimulus. 
In another 
series of experi- 
ments it was shown that following 
complete adaptation 
to 5 X lo-‘M 
CAMP, further 
secretion 
of [3H]cAMP 
could be elicited 
by changing 
the stimulus 
to 5 X lo-’ 
M CAMP plus folic acid (not shown). 
The above experiments 
demonstrate 
that folic acid 
plus a given concentration 
of CAMP is interpreted 
as 
a somewhat 
higher 
CAMP concentration-in 
terms of 
both activation 
of CAMP production 
and adaptation 
(Fig. 6). However, previous experiments 
suggested that 
folic acid alone elicits 
little 
adaptation 
(Figs. 4E, 5A, 
F). To test definitively 
whether 
folic 
acid elicits 
any 
adaptation, 
cells were pretreated 
with lop4 M folic acid 
and then tested with 
a low concentration 
of CAMP. It 
appears that even under rapid perfusion 
conditions, 
the 
low level of [3H]cAMP 
secreted induces some adapta- 
tion. This might 
explain 
why [3H]cAMP 
secretion 
re- 
sponses during folic acid applications 
are initially 
self- 
limiting 
but often spontaneously 
reoccur as the treat- 
ment is continued 
(Fig. 4E). This 
complication 
was 
minimized 
by two protocols 
used to test whether 
folic 
acid caused adaptation. 
In the first, perfusion 
with folic 
acid was continued 
well beyond the initial 
release of 
[3H]cAMP 
before the test CAMP stimulus 
was applied. 
In the second, the initial 
release of [3H]cAMP 
was 
blocked by caffeine 
(which 
is known 
not to block ad- 
aptation-Burton-Theibert 
and Devreotes, 
manuscript 
in preparation). 
As illustrated 
in Fig. ‘7, folic acid pre- 
treatment 
did not attenuate 
the subsequent response to 
CAMP in either protocol. 
It clearly 
did not reduce re- 
sponsiveness as would pretreatment 
with CAMP. 
Eflect of Folic Acid and Pteridine 
Analogs 
on CAMP Secretion 
Increases in the rate of secretion of [3H]cAMP 
could 
also be elicited by pteridine 
and folic acid analogs. Table 
1 summarizes 
these data. The effect of folic acid was 
dose dependent; 
with 
aggregation 
competent 
cells, it 
was first observed with 
lo-’ 
to lo-? M. The maximum 
effect occurred 
at 1O-4 to lop3 M although 
it was not 
clear 
the 
effect 
saturated. 
Xanthopterin 
(2-amino- 
4,6-dihydroxypteridine), 
pterine 
(2-amino-4-hydroxy- 
pteridine) 
and pterin-6-carboxylic 
acid were equally ef- 
fective 
as folic acid. PABA 
(paraaminobenzoic 
acid), 
160 
DEVELOPMENTALBIOLOGY 
lumazine 
(2,4-dihydroxypteridine), 
and folinic 
acid 
(5-formyl-tetrahydropteroylglutamic 
acid) were com- 
pletely ineffective. The quinazolines 
(5,LGdideazafolic 
acid and 5,8-dideazaisofolic acid) which lack the reduc- 
ible nitrogen atoms in the pteridine ring were partially 
effective. Since the pteridine ring is apparently the ac- 
tive portion of the folic acid molecule, it was surprising 
that folate tetraglutamate 
was completely inactive. 
Amethopterine 
(4-amino-lo-methyl 
pteroylglutamic 
acid) and aminopterine (4-amino pteroylglutamic 
acid) 
were less than 20% as effective as folic acid at the high- 
est concentrations 
tested (10e4 M). Amethopterine 
blocked the effect of folic acid when the two were added 
together, but this inhibition 
was readily reversible. 
When the perfusion solution was switched from 1O-4 M 
amethopterine to 10e4 M folic acid, the effect of folic 
acid was similar to a control not pretreated with ame- 
thopterin. 
DISCUSSION 
It has been demonstrated that folic acid potentiates 
CAMP synthesis and secretion in response to CAMP 
stimuli (Fig. 6); the increase elicited by folic acid plus 
CAMP was often greater than the sum of the increases 
elicited by each chemoattractant 
individually 
(Fig. 4C). 
In contrast, folic acid presented singly as stimulus (i.e., 
under conditions of rapid perfusion in which CAMP can- 
not serve as a secondary stimulus) elicits only a min- 
imal increase in the rate of [3H]cAMP secretion (Figs. 
3A, 4B). Thus, the large response (i.e., elevation in cel- 
lular CAMP) elicited by folic acid in cell suspensions is 
most likely due to the enhancement by folic acid of a 
response to secreted CAMP (Wurster 
et al., 19’78; 
Fig. 2). 
Often two to three times more CAMP was released 
when folic acid was added to a high CAMP stimulus 
(Figs 2, 4D, F, 6). This suggests that the highest doses 
of CAMP do not maximally activate the adenylate cy- 
clase. It was also noted that folic acid increased the 
duration of the response to maximal doses of CAMP 
(Figs. 4D, F, 6A-C). This again indicates that folic acid 
plus CAMP is interpreted 
as a larger stimulus since 
progressively larger CAMP stimuli elicit longer respon- 
ses (Devreotes and Steck, 1979). 
Kawai (1980) has demonstrated that folic acid rapidly 
increases the binding of [3H]cAMP to both vegetative 
and aggregation competent cells of D. discoideum. Folic 
acid had similar effects on binding of lo-’ M and lo-’ 
M[3H]cAMP, suggesting that the number of sites, not 
the affinity, increased. Such an effect would be consis- 
tent with the results presented here. Folic acid, by sud- 
denly increasing in the number of CAMP binding sites 
available to background CAMP, could initiate

prepared 
and rates 
of

measured

under 
Methods. 
Eight 
identical 
sets of cells are represented; 
experiment 
illustrated 
in each 
of four panels was carried 
out in duplicate 
(duplicate 
symbols). 
In 
panels B and D, 10e4 M folic 
acid was introduced

(Fl) 
and withdrawn

synthesis and secretion (Figs. 2, 3, 4B, E, 5A, F, 7B). 
Added together with a given CAMP stimulus, it would 
potentiate CAMP-stimulated 
CAMP synthesis and se- 
cretion by increasing the amount of bound CAMP (Figs. 
4C, F, 6). Switching the stimulus from CAMP to folic 
acid would lead to abrupt termination 
of the response 
(Fig. 5); the decrease in the number of occupied CAMP 
binding sites upon removal of CAMP would be more 
significant than the increased availability 
of sites pro- 
vided by folic acid. However, a second increase in 
[3H]cAMP secretion is expected if the stimulus 
is 
switched to folic acid plus CAMP (Fig. 6B). Although 
the data presented here support the possibility 
that 
folic acid increases CAMP binding to the cell surface, 
other mechanisms are also possible. In fact, preliminary 
attempts to repeat a Kawai’s (1980) observation have 
been unsuccessful. Folic acid could also act by inhibiting 
a vital phosphodiesterase although no effect was found 
on either the cell membrane phosphodiesterase or total 
cellular phosphodiesterase (data not shown). 
In each of these proposed mechanisms a response to 
folic acid occurs only in the presence of CAMP. That is, 
folic acid appears to potentiate a pathway initiated by 
the binding of CAMP. The slight effects of folic acid

alone on rates of [3H]cAMP 
secretion 
under rapid per- 
fusion conditions 
are most likely 
due to enhancement 
of a response 
to residual 
low background 
levels 
of 
CAMP. (The greater 
than additive 
effect of the mixed 
stimuli 
also supports 
this conclusion.) 
However, 
since 
this slight 
effect of folilc acid could not be completely 
eliminated 
(Figs. 3A, C, 4B, 7B), the possibility 
that it 
directly 
elicits a small increase in CAMP synthesis 
and 
secretion cannot be rule’d out. If this were the case, the 
effect of lop3 M folic acid would be equivalent 
to that 
of about 10~s-lO-s M CAMP. In addition 
whereas pre- 
treatment 
with CAMP Causes adaptation 
to both CAMP 
and folic acid; pretreatment 
with 
folic 
acid does not 
decrease subsequent 
responsiveness 
to CAMP. 
Bonner et al. (1970) reported 
that the folic acid po- 
tentiated 
the chemotactic 
response 
to low doses of 
CAMP. Is the effect of folic acid as a chemoattractant 
merely 
an enhancement 
of CAMP-mediated 
chemo- 
taxis? To examine 
this possibility 
the light-scattering 
response (presumably 
a. reflection 
of the chemotactic 
response) elicited 
by folic acid in suspensions 
of Ax-3 
cells was examined. 
Advantage 
was taken of the ob- 
servation 
that 2 mM caffeine inhibits 
CAMP secretion 
but has little 
effect on thle light-scattering 
response (M. 
Brenner, 
personal 
communication). 
It was found that 
folic acid-elicited 
light-scattering 
responses were un- 
affected by caffeine. These preliminary 
results suggest 
that while 
the majority 
of the effect of folic acid on 
CAMP production 
depends on extracellular 
CAMP, its 
effect on light 
scattering 
is not due to a secondary re- 
sponse to secreted CAMP (experiment 
carried out with 
M. Brenner). 
This result is puzzling 
since the studies reported here 
with 
structural 
analogs of folic acid suggest that the 
site involved 
in folic acid-directed 
chemotaxis 
is similar 
to that which mediates the effects of folic acid on CAMP 
synthesis 
and secretion. 
For stimulation 
of CAMP pro- 
duction, the pteridine 
ring appears to be the active por- 
tion of the molecule 
since pterine 
and xanthopterine 
are equally 
as effective as folic acid while 
paraamino- 
benzoyl 
glutamate 
is inactive. 
The Z-position 
amino 
group appears essential for activity 
since lumazine 
was 
ineffective. 
Previous work on chemotaxis 
led to similar 
conclusions 
(Pan and Wurster, 
1978). Folinic 
acid had 
no effect on CAMP synthesis 
and secretion 
suggesting 
that the 5-position 
of the pteridine 
ring is essential for 
this activity. 
Although 
folinic 
acid is reduced, quinazo- 
line analogs (which 
cannot be reduced) were partially 
active (see Table 1). It will 
be interesting 
if a similar 
pattern 
of activity 
is found for chemotaxis. 
Folic acid apparently 
serves as a chemoattractant 
for 
cells in the early stages of development 
and also affects 
CAMP production 
and secretion 
in aggregation 
com- 
petent cells, suggesting 
that the intracellular 
pathways 
mediating 
chemotaxis 
and CAMP signaling 
are related. 
The simplest 
possibility 
would be that a CAMP and a 
folic acid receptor 
each can stimulate 
each pathway 
(Bernstein 
et al., 1981). However, 
the results presented 
here suggest that folic acid and CAMP do not affect 
CAMP production 
by identical 
mechanisms. 
There is 
only a minimal 
activation 
of CAMP production 
by folic 
acid, it does not appear to elicit 
adaptation 
as does 
CAMP, and it appears to potentiate 
CAMP-mediated 
CAMP production. 
Studies are in progress to elucidate 
these differences 
and to further 
understand 
the rela- 
tionship 
of the chemotactic 
and CAMP-signaling 
path- 
ways.
